attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Labour Ward in active labour at 39 weeks gestation. She has had limited antenatal care. She has a medical history of chronic Hepatitis C infection, diagnosed five years ago. She has received treatment for Hepatitis C prior to pregnancy but has not had a recent viral load performed. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals a term pregnancy with cephalic presentation. Vaginal examination confirms she is 5 cm dilated with intact membranes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan for this patient's labour and delivery?
options: Use additional personal protective equipment beyond universal precautions
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a woman in active labour with known chronic Hepatitis C infection. The most appropriate management plan is to proceed with standard labour management. Hepatitis C is not a contraindication to vaginal delivery, and no special precautions beyond standard universal precautions are necessary during labour and delivery.

Performing an emergency caesarean section is not indicated in this case. Hepatitis C infection is not an obstetric indication for caesarean delivery. Studies have shown that the mode of delivery does not significantly impact the risk of vertical transmission of Hepatitis C.

Administering intravenous antiviral therapy during labour is not recommended for Hepatitis C. Unlike HIV, where intrapartum antiretroviral therapy can reduce vertical transmission, there is no evidence that antiviral therapy during labour reduces Hepatitis C transmission to the newborn.

Using additional personal protective equipment beyond universal precautions is unnecessary. Standard universal precautions, which should be used for all patients regardless of known infection status, are sufficient for managing labour and delivery in women with Hepatitis C.

Avoiding artificial rupture of membranes is not a recommended practice for Hepatitis C-positive mothers. While prolonged rupture of membranes may slightly increase the risk of vertical transmission, artificial rupture of membranes is not contraindicated and can be performed if clinically indicated.

It's important to note that vertical transmission of Hepatitis C occurs in approximately 5% of pregnancies, primarily at the time of delivery. However, this risk does not warrant altering standard obstetric practices. After delivery, the infant should be tested for Hepatitis C, but breastfeeding is not contraindicated. The mother should be referred for Hepatitis C treatment postpartum, as newer direct-acting antivirals have high cure rates and are contraindicated during pregnancy due to limited safety data.
learningPoints: Universal precautions are sufficient for managing labour in women with Hepatitis C; no additional measures are required.
Hepatitis C is not a contraindication to vaginal delivery or breastfeeding.
Vertical transmission of Hepatitis C occurs in approximately 5% of pregnancies, primarily at the time of delivery.
references: Australasian Society for Infectious Diseases (ASID). Management of perinatal infections. Melbourne: The Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG); 2014. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/ASID-Management-of-Perinatal-Infections.pdf
